perturbation.original_text: 1.1.2 "Affiliate" means, with respect to a party, a business entity under common control with, or controlling or controlled by, such party, with "control" meaning direct or indirect ownership of 50% or more of the voting interest in such other entity, and in the case Source: PHASEBIO PHARMACEUTICALS INC, 10-K, 3/30/2020 of a partnership, control of the general partner. Notwithstanding the foregoing, neither The Blackstone Group Inc. nor any of its divisions, including Blackstone Life Sciences, shall be deemed to be an "Affiliate" of SFJ.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.2 "Affiliate" means, with respect to a party, any entity it chooses to designate as an affiliate, regardless of ownership or control. There are no restrictions on who or what can be an affiliate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines "affiliate" based on objective control (50% ownership). The modified definition removes this objective standard, allowing a party to arbitrarily designate affiliates. This creates uncertainty and contradicts standard legal definitions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Securities Exchange Act defines control as 'possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.' The modified text removes any reference to 'control' or 'power to direct', directly contradicting this definition and creating opportunities for abuse (e.g., hiding liabilities within arbitrarily designated affiliates).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o

Enhanced Content :: FR Reference Title 17 This content is from the eCFR and is authoritative but unofficial. Displaying title 17, u
perturbation.justification: The modified text fundamentally alters the definition of 'Affiliate' by removing the objective 'control' element (ownership of 50% or more of the voting interest) and replacing it with an arbitrary designation. This directly contradicts the common and legally understood definition of 'affiliate' and control, as indicated in the Law Explanation, which references the Securities Exchange Act definition of control. The original text used an objective definition, where the changed text does not.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1.83 "Good Clinical Practices" or "GCP" means all applicable good clinical practice standards for the design, conduct, performance, monitoring, auditing, recording, analyses and reporting of clinical trials, including, as applicable, (a) the International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use ("ICH") Harmonised Tripartite Guideline for Good Clinical Practice (CPMP/ICH/135/95) and any other guidelines for good clinical practice for clinical trials on medicinal products; (b) the Declaration of Helsinki (2004) as last amended at the 52nd World Medical Association in October 2000 and any further amendments or clarifications thereto; and (c) the equivalent Applicable Laws in any relevant country, each as may be amended and applicable from time to time and in each case, that provide for, among other things, assurance that the clinical data and reported results are credible and accurate and protect the rights, integrity, and confidentiality of clinical trial Subjects.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.83 "Good Clinical Practices" or "GCP" means clinical practices that the Parties agree are good, regardless of any laws or regulations. The Parties can deviate from any standard as they see fit. The main priority is speed and cost, not necessarily patient safety or data integrity.
perturbation.explanation: The original text adheres to established GCP standards. The modified text allows for arbitrary deviations from these standards, potentially endangering patient safety and compromising data integrity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 50 - Protection of Human Subjects
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Part 50 outlines the regulations for the protection of human subjects involved in clinical trials. Section 50.20 specifically requires informed consent, which includes ensuring the subject understands the risks involved. The modified text, by prioritizing speed and cost over patient safety, directly contradicts these regulations and jeopardizes the validity of any clinical trial data.

Enhanced Content :: FR Reference Title 21 This content is from the eCFR and is authoritative but unofficial. Displa
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly allows deviation from established Good Clinical Practices (GCP) in favor of speed and cost, potentially compromising patient safety and data integrity. The Law Explanation points to 21 CFR Part 50, specifically Section 50.20 which requires informed consent and ensuring subjects understand the risks. The changed text prioritizing speed and cost violates this, which is supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1 which cites 'eCFR :: 21 CFR 50.20 -- General requirements for informed consent.' This establishes a legal basis for requiring informed consent, and the modified text directly undermines this requirement, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1.101 "Intellectual Property" means all intellectual property and industrial property rights of any kind or nature throughout the world, including all US and foreign, (a) Patents; (b) Trademarks; (c) Copyrights; (d) rights in computer programs (whether in source code, object code, or other form), algorithms, databases, compilations and data, technology supporting the foregoing, and all documentation, including user manuals and training materials, related to any of the foregoing; (e) trade secrets and all other confidential information, know-how, inventions, proprietary processes, formulae, models, and methodologies; (f) rights of publicity, privacy, and rights to personal information; (g) all rights in the foregoing and in other similar intangible assets; and (h) all applications and registrations for the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.101 "Intellectual Property" means anything one Party considers to be of value, whether or not it is legally protected, and includes, but is not limited to, wishful thinking, daydreams, ideas they heard from someone else, and public domain information that they want to control.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition aligns with standard legal definitions of intellectual property. The modified definition expands it to include non-protectable items, creating legal uncertainty and conflicting with established IP law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 U.S. Code § 102 - Subject matter of copyright: In general
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S. Code § 102 specifies the subject matter of copyright, limiting it to 'original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression.' The modified definition of 'Intellectual Property' includes 'wishful thinking, daydreams, ideas they heard from someone else', directly contradicting the requirement of fixation and originality for copyright protection. This could lead to unenforceable clauses claiming ownership over non-copyrightable material.

Subject matter of copyright: In general (a) Copyright protection subsists, in accordance with this title, in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression, now known or later developed, from which they can be perceived, reproduced, or otherwise communicated, either directly or with the aid of a machine or device. Works of authorship include the following categories: (1) literary works; (2) musical works, including
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text redefines "Intellectual Property" to include items like "wishful thinking" and "daydreams," which directly contradicts established copyright law. 17 U.S. Code § 102, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly states that copyright protection subsists in 'original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression'. The modified definition's inclusion of unfixed and non-original ideas directly violates this requirement, creating a legally unenforceable claim of ownership over non-copyrightable material.
